What's behind it
Market signal (MMI) GREEN +0.59
Macro stress CALM 1.9
MRMI = MMI + macro buffer. Posture is LONG above +0.25, CAUTION from −0.50 to +0.25, CASH below −0.50.
This week’s read

MRMI jumped from -0.06 to +0.25 this week — exactly at the LONG threshold, still in CAUTION at 75% exposure, but the move provides a direct answer to last week's central question: equities were more right than the bond market. May CPI's soft core print (+0.2% month-over-month) defused the rate-repricing thesis, and the U.S.-Iran ceasefire then removed the energy supply shock driving the hot headline number, sending Brent crude down more than 5% and making the +4.2% year-over-year print look backward-looking almost immediately. That geopolitical relief did most of the work on the market side: MMI moved from +0.25 to +0.59 as financial conditions recovered +0.73 and breadth surged +0.74 on the broad rally — the kind of nearly universal sector participation that tells the model the move has width, not just noise. The economy pillar barely shifted — the macro buffer slipped from +0.44 to +0.41 and stress rose to +1.86, still firmly Calm — but what's underneath is more two-sided than before: growth is recovering (Real Economy Score from -0.98 to -0.46, GDPNow back to 3.3%) while the inflation direction axis warmed from +0.04 to +0.30, meaning the backdrop is stabilizing rather than cleanly improving. MRMI is parked at the exact number separating CAUTION from LONG, which puts the FOMC decision and dot plot tomorrow — Chair Warsh's debut — in a live position: a balanced hold with unchanged projections lets the financial conditions recovery consolidate and gives the lagging growth impulse index time to catch up, which likely tips MRMI into LONG; a harder neutral signal reprices bond volatility fast, partially unwinds the MMI gain, and pushes MRMI back toward mid-CAUTION. Beyond tomorrow, the watch is the inflation direction reading: at +0.30 and drifting warmer, two more months of tariff pass-through showing up in core goods would start eroding the macro buffer from the inside — and that's the scenario where MRMI's path to a sustained LONG narrows even if equity markets stay calm.

1How the index has evolved

Milk Road Macro Index (MRMI)

How it's built: MRMI combines MMI (market momentum from credit, breadth and volatility) with a macro buffer that erodes when growth and inflation both deteriorate. The formula is unchanged; the production posture layer maps MRMI to LONG, CAUTION, or CASH.

Reading the chart: white line is the MRMI value over time. Green zone = LONG above +0.25, amber = CAUTION from −0.50 to +0.25, red = CASH below −0.50. Toggle assets in the legend to overlay SPX / Russell / BTC.

Next: open MMI Drivers below to see what's behind the market signal, then Macro Backdrop for the economy signal.

An allocation posture index: LONG above +0.25, CAUTION (75% exposure) from −0.50 to +0.25, CASH below −0.50.

2What the markets are signalingMarket pillar

MMI — Market Momentum Index. The fast, market-derived half of MRMI: built entirely from price and volatility data — credit spreads, cyclical sector breadth, and financial-conditions volatility — equally weighted. Markets react quickly, so MMI catches turning points early; the trade-off is they can also flash false alarms, which is why MMI alone never triggers a CASH call. It only acts in concert with the slower economy pillar below.

Market Momentum Index (MMI)

What you're seeing: the MMI score over time, equally-weighted from GII (global growth momentum), Breadth (cyclical participation) and FinCon (financial conditions). Above zero = healthy momentum. Below zero = momentum is rolling over.

Drivers below: click any row to expand the underlying series. The MMI score is just the average of the three.

Market momentum on its own, averaged from credit spreads, sector breadth and financial-conditions volatility.

This week’s read · market pillar

MMI moved from +0.25 — last week's knife-edge hold on the LONG/CAUTION line — to +0.59, settling firmly in the green as two of its three drivers surged together over the week. Financial conditions jumped +0.73 to +0.62, powered by VIX falling from 18.80 to 17.68 and bond volatility (MOVE) dropping sharply from 75.20 to 69.36; breadth ran +0.74 to +1.46 as the U.S.-Iran ceasefire announced Sunday June 14 triggered a sweeping Monday rally — Dow to a fresh record, nearly every sector participating — exactly the kind of broad move that lifts both volatility and breadth readings at once. May CPI came in at +4.2% year-over-year and initially fed the "rates held higher" narrative from last week's payrolls, but the market absorbed it with little damage: the headline was almost entirely energy-driven, core CPI printed a soft +0.2% month-over-month, and the ceasefire — by reopening the Strait of Hormuz and sending Brent crude down more than 5% — immediately undercut the supply shock behind that hot number. GII, the growth impulse index, is the lone drag: it fell -0.44 over the week to -0.31, with an additional -0.42 move on the day, suggesting that credit, copper, and rotation signals haven't yet caught up to the volatility compression — these tend to lag the headline equity moves and may take a few sessions to confirm. The outstanding risk is the FOMC decision and dot plot on June 17, new Chair Warsh's debut: the hold is essentially certain, but if the updated projections shift the 2026 median to zero cuts and the press conference signals a harder neutral stance, bond volatility would reprice fast and could reverse a meaningful chunk of the FinCon improvement that drove most of MMI's gain this week.

MMI DRIVERS · GII · Breadth · FinCon — click any row to expand
3What the economy is signalingEconomy pillar

Macro Stress. The slow, economy-derived half of MRMI: built from real-economy data — consumer spending, jobs, income, GDP nowcast — and inflation trajectory. The unified 0–10 score is calm by default, rises when either growth weakens or inflation accelerates, and builds fastest when both factors hit together.

Macro Stress

What you're seeing: Martin's unified macro-stress score on a 0–10 scale. It is calm by default, rises when growth weakens or inflation accelerates, and builds fastest when both hit together.

MRMI buffer: this same stress score now erodes the macro buffer used in the production MRMI strategy.

Inputs below: the driver rows show the raw axes: Real Economy Score and Inflation Direction Δ6m.

1.9 CALM 0–10 score

Stagflation pressure — growth weakness × inflation rising. Calm by default, builds when both factors hit.

MACRO STRESS INPUTS · Real Economy Score · Inflation Direction Δ6m — click any row to expand
This week’s read · economy pillar

The stress score moved to +1.86 this week — up +0.68, but still firmly Calm — and the macro buffer feeding MRMI barely budged, slipping from +0.44 last week to +0.41. The previous brief flagged May CPI as the key swing factor; it landed on June 10 and gave a split verdict. Core came in at +0.2% month-over-month, a small downside surprise against the +0.3% consensus, which is why core CPI YoY held at 2.90% and the buffer didn't crater. But the six-month directional change on core inflation is no longer near-flat: it accelerated from +0.04 pp to +0.30 pp, meaning inflation is slowly tilting warmer on the trajectory the framework watches most closely, and that's what pushed stress higher. On the other side of the ledger, the real-economy picture improved sharply: the Real Economy Score climbed from -0.98 to -0.46 (+0.53 on the week), and Atlanta Fed GDPNow edged back up toward +3.3% annualized for Q2 — spending and investment holding up well enough to partially offset the soft-jobs-composition story from the prior brief. The offsetting moves — warming inflation direction, recovering growth — largely netted out, leaving the stress score modestly higher but the Calm reading intact and the MRMI buffer still near full strength. What would shift this next: the FOMC decision and dot-plot update tomorrow (June 17) could matter if Chair Warsh signals fewer cuts or a higher-for-longer bias, but the bigger test for the dashboard is whether the inflation direction axis keeps drifting positive over the next two CPI prints — if July and August data confirm that tariff pass-through is accelerating into core goods, the +0.30 reading could push toward the level where it starts meaningfully eroding the buffer and lifting stress toward the Watch band.

IndicatorCategoryLatestNotes
US M2 Money SupplyUS M2 Money Supply (FRED M2SL), shown as YoY % growth. Cross-currency global M2 would also include ECB / BOJ / PBOC — US M2 is the dominant component. Liquidity +3.9% Monetary fuel for risk assets · expanding = tailwind
US M2 Money Supply (FRED M2SL), shown as YoY % growth. Cross-currency global M2 would also include ECB / BOJ / PBOC — US M2 is the dominant component.
ISM Manufacturing PMIISM Manufacturing PMI (diffusion index; >50 = manufacturing expansion, <50 = contraction), sourced from the Institute for Supply Management via DBnomics mirror because FRED's legacy NAPM CSV endpoint now returns 404. Activity 48.70 Institute for Supply Management · DBnomics mirror
ISM Manufacturing PMI (diffusion index; >50 = manufacturing expansion, <50 = contraction), sourced from the Institute for Supply Management via DBnomics mirror because FRED's legacy NAPM CSV endpoint now returns 404.
Atlanta Fed GDPNowAtlanta Fed GDPNow — real-time nowcast of current-quarter Real GDP growth (annualized %). Updates several times per quarter as new data prints. Activity +3.29% Closes the publication-lag gap on official GDP
Atlanta Fed GDPNow — real-time nowcast of current-quarter Real GDP growth (annualized %). Updates several times per quarter as new data prints.
CFNAIChicago Fed National Activity Index — composite of 85 monthly US activity indicators, normalized to mean 0. Above 0 = above-trend growth, below 0 = below-trend. Activity 0.14 Comprehensive cross-sector US activity composite
Chicago Fed National Activity Index — composite of 85 monthly US activity indicators, normalized to mean 0. Above 0 = above-trend growth, below 0 = below-trend.
Industrial ProductionIndustrial Production index (manufacturing + mining + utilities), shown as YoY % change. Long historical series, biased toward goods-producing sectors. Activity +1.2% Goods-side activity · partial overlap with copper
Industrial Production index (manufacturing + mining + utilities), shown as YoY % change. Long historical series, biased toward goods-producing sectors.
Housing StartsHousing Starts (thousands of units, SAAR). Rate-sensitive — tends to lead the cycle when the Fed pivots. Activity 1K Rate-sensitive · leads the cycle on Fed pivots
Housing Starts (thousands of units, SAAR). Rate-sensitive — tends to lead the cycle when the Fed pivots.
Building PermitsBuilding Permits (thousands of units, SAAR). Leads housing starts by 1–2 months — even more forward-looking on construction activity. Activity 1K Permits lead starts by 1–2 months
Building Permits (thousands of units, SAAR). Leads housing starts by 1–2 months — even more forward-looking on construction activity.
Official CPI HeadlineOfficial headline Consumer Price Index for All Urban Consumers (FRED CPIAUCSL), shown as YoY % inflation. Includes food and energy. Inflation +3.9% BLS/FRED · headline consumer inflation YoY
Official headline Consumer Price Index for All Urban Consumers (FRED CPIAUCSL), shown as YoY % inflation. Includes food and energy.
Official Core CPIOfficial Core CPI excluding food and energy (FRED CPILFENS), shown as YoY % inflation. This is the dashboard's inflation-direction anchor. Inflation +2.6% BLS/FRED · core consumer inflation YoY
Official Core CPI excluding food and energy (FRED CPILFENS), shown as YoY % inflation. This is the dashboard's inflation-direction anchor.
Official PPI All CommoditiesOfficial Producer Price Index by Commodity: All Commodities (FRED PPIACO), shown as YoY %. Broad upstream producer-price pressure, more volatile than CPI. Inflation +12.3% BLS/FRED · broad producer prices YoY
Official Producer Price Index by Commodity: All Commodities (FRED PPIACO), shown as YoY %. Broad upstream producer-price pressure, more volatile than CPI.
Initial Jobless ClaimsInitial unemployment insurance claims (weekly, thousands). Highest-frequency real-economy signal in the framework — recession indicator when the 4-week moving average rises sharply. Labor 229K Weekly · most reliable recession indicator
Initial unemployment insurance claims (weekly, thousands). Highest-frequency real-economy signal in the framework — recession indicator when the 4-week moving average rises sharply.
Continuing Jobless ClaimsContinuing unemployment insurance claims (weekly, thousands). Tracks how long workers stay unemployed — rising trend can signal labor-market deterioration even when initial claims are low. Labor 1,795K Companion to initial claims · tracks duration of unemployment
Continuing unemployment insurance claims (weekly, thousands). Tracks how long workers stay unemployed — rising trend can signal labor-market deterioration even when initial claims are low.
Library entries don't drive the headline posture — they're context that explains narrative shifts. Most need data wiring before they can populate.
